entry.title: Scaling Generative AI in SAP S/4HANA: Enterprise-Ready Innovation with ISLM
entry.content:
• Introduction and context
  • Key Enhancements for Generative AI Enablement in ISLM
  • Availability and Downport Strategy Across SAP S/4HANA Releases
  • Deep Dive: Latest ISLM Capabilities for Generative AI
  • Orchestration Service Integration
  • Developing Custom Generative AI Use Cases — Step-by-Step guide
  • Support for new models offered by Generative AI Hub
  • Developing Your Own AI-Enabled Applications in SAP ABAP Cloud with ISLM and ABAP AI SDK
  • Conclusion

Introduction and context

In today’s rapidly evolving digital landscape, Generative AI  is transforming how enterprises innovate, automate, and deliver business value . To successfully embed Generative AI within SAP S/4HANA, organizations need more than access to large language models. They require a structured, scalable, and governed framework to integrate, operate, and manage AI use cases across their landscape.

Over the past year, these Generative AI capabilities are now enabled through ISLM, helping customers and partners move from experimentation to productive usage. You can learn more through my previous blog post and the  YouTube webinar replay which outline the architecture, supported use cases, and how ISLM integrates Generative AI into SAP S/4HANA SAP business applications. 

Building on this foundation, this blog focuses on the latest and upcoming enhancements in Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management that further strengthen Generative AI enablement. We will explore what’s new, how these enhancements simplify GenAI integration and lifecycle management, and how customers and partners can leverage them to accelerate innovation and scale AI-driven business scenarios in SAP S/4HANA.

Key Enhancements for Generative AI Enablement in ISLM

  1. Integration with the Orchestration Service:  ISLM now integrates with the Orchestration Service to deliver advanced, enterprise-grade  capabilities for Generative AI scenarios. This includes Document Grounding to enrich prompts with business-relevant context, Content Filtering to ensure safe and compliant responses, and Data Masking to protect sensitive information before it is processed by large language models. In addition, the service supports input and output translation for multilingual use cases and incorporates a Prompt Shield filter to help defend against prompt injection attacks. Together, these capabilities significantly enhance the security, reliability, and governance of Generative AI embedded within SAP business applications.
  2. Support for new models offered by Generative AI Hub: ISLM enables customers to leverage state-of-the-art large language models while maintaining flexibility and future readiness.
  3. Reuse Connectivity to Gen AI Hub:  A shared connectivity model allows a single connection between the ABAP system and the SAP Generative AI Hub to be configured once and reused across multiple AI use cases. This simplifies administration, improves governance, and reduces setup effort.
  4. Simplification of creation of Intelligent Scenario: Creation of Intelligent Scenarios has been simplified, moving from a pro-code approach to a more streamlined low-code experience.In addition, ISLM allows the optional use of the prediction class for Generative AI scenarios. 

Availability and Downport Strategy Across SAP S/4HANA Releases 

To accelerate the adoption of Generative AI, the latest ISLM enhancements have been downported to lower SAP S/4HANA releases, enabling customers to benefit from new capabilities without waiting for a full system upgrade. These enhancements are available for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise.

Supported Releases (No TCI Required)
For the following Support Package (SP) levels, the latest capabilities are already included — no additional TCI implementation is required:

  • SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS01
  • SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS06

Lower SP Levels (TCI Required)
For lower SP levels within the 2025 and 2023 releases, the relevant Transport-based Correction Instructions (TCIs) must be implemented to enable the latest ISLM capabilities.

By delivering these enhancements via TCIs, customers can adopt the newest ISLM innovations without requiring a complete Support Package update. This approach significantly reduces upgrade complexity while providing faster access to the latest Generative AI functionality in ISLM.

To enable the new capabilities, please implement the corresponding SAP Notes listed below.

  • SAP Note 3682148
  • SAP Note 3668194

Deep Dive: Latest ISLM Capabilities for Generative AI

Orchestration Service Integration

ISLM supports integration with the Orchestration Service on SAP AI Core, enabling advanced Generative AI capabilities such as document grounding, content filtering, and data masking. Additional features like input and output translation and a Prompt Shield filter help protect against prompt injection attacks, strengthening security. New API enhancements further improve grounding quality through larger chunk sizes and application-specific document chunking. Together, these capabilities enable standardized, flexible, and enterprise-ready GenAI integration—allowing customers to switch between foundation models without code changes and reinforcing ISLM as the central framework for governing and scaling Generative AI across SAP landscapes.

The following modules within the orchestration service are supported by ISLM :

  1. Data Masking – Protects sensitive information by masking or removing confidential data.
  2. Input Filtering – Validates and sanitizes incoming data before it is processed by the LLM.
  3. Output Filtering – Reviews and filters the LLM-generated output to ensure compliance and relevance.
  4. Grounding – Aligns model outputs with enterprise data or domain-specific knowledge to improve response accuracy.
Developing Custom Generative AI Use Cases — Step-by-Step guide

Following information is relevant for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition & On-Premise releases 
For step-by-step guide on using orchestration within ISLM, let’s consider the following example use case:

Use Case: AI-Driven Return Policy Analysis with ISLM

In this scenario, two fictional sports brands — Company Alpha and Company Beta — maintain different return policies with varying eligibility rules, timelines, refund conditions, and exceptions.

Customer support teams frequently handle queries such as:

  • “Is this item still eligible for return?”
  • “Are customized products refundable?”
  • “How long will the refund take?”

Manually reviewing policy documents is time-consuming and can lead to inconsistent responses.
Using ISLM with orchestration: 

  • Policy documents are uploaded for document grounding.
  • User queries are processed through the orchestration workflow.
  • The LLM extracts relevant clauses and generates structured responses.

Business Value

  • Reduces manual effort
  • Speeds up response time
  • Ensures consistent policy interpretation
  • Enables automated decision support within business processes

Implementation Steps 

  1. Set and configure SAP AI Core
  2. Design and development of the Orchestration Workflow
  3. Export workflow configuration
  4. Create and deploy Intelligent Scenarios 
  5. Consumption of the Intelligent Scenario with ABAP AI SDK APIs

Prerequisites for using Gen AI scenario in ISLM. Ensure that you have completed the following steps before continuing with creating your own Gen AI scenario: Prerequisites | SAP Help Portal

1) Access AI Launchpad: Design and Development of the Orchestration Workflow
Open an available instance of the SAP Generative AI Hub – >AI Launchpad to start prompt engineering and configure your orchestration workflow.

1.1) Configure Orchestration Modules
Within the AI Launchpad, configure the required modules for the orchestration service according to your use case, refer to the following link:  Build Your Orchestration Workflow | SAP Help Portal

Sample Orchestration Configuration 

{
	"modules": {
		"grounding": {
			"type": "document_grounding_service",
			"config": {
				"filters": [],
				"placeholders": {
					"input": [
						"grounding_input_variable_1"
					],
					"output": "grounding_output_variable"
				}
			}
		},
		"prompt_templating": {
			"prompt": {
				"template": [
					{
						"role": "user",
						"content": [
							{
								"type": "text",
								"text": "{{?grounding_output_variable}}"
							}
						]
					}
				],
				"defaults": {}
			},
			"model": {
				"name": "gpt-5-mini",
				"params": {
					"max_tokens": 64000,
					"temperature": 1
				},
				"version": "1"
			}
		},
		"filtering": {
			"input": {
				"filters": [
					{
						"type": "azure_content_safety",
						"config": {
							"hate": 2,
							"self_harm": 2,
							"sexual": 2,
							"violence": 2
						}
					},
					{
						"type": "llama_guard_3_8b",
						"config": {
							"child_exploitation": true,
							"code_interpreter_abuse": true,
							"defamation": true,
							"elections": true,
							"hate": true,
							"indiscriminate_weapons": true,
							"intellectual_property": true,
							"non_violent_crimes": true,
							"privacy": true,
							"self_harm": true,
							"sex_crimes": true,
							"sexual_content": true,
							"specialized_advice": true,
							"violent_crimes": true
						}
					}
				]
			},
			"output": {
				"filters": [
					{
						"type": "azure_content_safety",
						"config": {
							"hate": 2,
							"self_harm": 2,
							"sexual": 2,
							"violence": 2
						}
					},
					{
						"type": "llama_guard_3_8b",
						"config": {
							"child_exploitation": true,
							"code_interpreter_abuse": true,
							"defamation": true,
							"elections": true,
							"hate": true,
							"indiscriminate_weapons": true,
							"intellectual_property": true,
							"non_violent_crimes": true,
							"privacy": true,
							"self_harm": true,
							"sex_crimes": true,
							"sexual_content": true,
							"specialized_advice": true,
							"violent_crimes": true
						}
					}
				]
			}
		},
		"masking": {
			"masking_providers": [
				{
					"type": "sap_data_privacy_integration",
					"method": "anonymization",
					"entities": [
						{
							"type": "profile-credit-card-number"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-driverlicense"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-email"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-sensitive-data"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-ethnicity"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-gender"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-pronouns-gender"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-iban"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-location"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-nationalid"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-nationality"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-org"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-passport"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-person"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-phone"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-political-group"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-sapids-public"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-religious-group"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-sapids-internal"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-ssn"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-sexual-orientation"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-trade-union"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-address"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-url"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-university"
						},
						{
							"type": "profile-username-password"
						}
					],
					"mask_grounding_input": {
						"enabled": true
					},
					"allowlist": []
				}
			]
		},
		"translation": {
			"input": {
				"type": "sap_document_translation",
				"config": {
					"source_language": "",
					"target_language": "en-US"
				}
			},
			"output": {
				"type": "sap_document_translation",
				"config": {
					"source_language": "",
					"target_language": "USER_LANG"
				}
			}
		}
	}
}

It is mandatory to have a valid orchestration configuration as a workflow. Ensure the orchestration workflow is configured and exported as a JSON file. Refer to the above section.

1.2) Export Workflow Configuration
After all necessary modules have been configured, export the orchestration workflow configuration as a JSON file for use or deployment.

2) Reuse Connectivity

A shared connectivity model allows a single connection between the ABAP system and the SAP Generative AI Hub to be configured once and reused across multiple AI use cases. This simplifies administration, improves governance, and reduces setup effort.

Maintain Intelligent Scenario Usage Type Connections | SAP Help Portal

 3) Create the Intelligent Scenario

3.1) Launch the SAP Fiori Launchpad and open the Intelligent Scenarios app.

Choose Create → Side-by-Side, then in the Settings tab:

  • Enter name and description
  • Select Generative AI as the Scenario Type
  • ISLM allows optional use of the prediction class for Generative AI scenarios 
  • Select Stateless – Customer as the Usage Type, which points to the reuse connectivity created in the previous step.

SAP Help Portal | SAP Online Help

 3.2)  Add Generative AI Model to the Intelligent scenario

Choose Add Model and maintain the required details:

  • Enter a model name and description
  • Select the appropriate Executable ID and corresponding model
  • (Optional) Choose a specific model version (latest is selected by default)
  • Optionally add a prompt template
  • Choose Add to confirm

3.3) Intelligent scenario with Orchestration service

    • Upload  the orchestration Execution Flow JSON in this model to integrate the orchestration service.
    • Use the Upload button to add the execution flow template or Copy the JSON into the dialog.  

  • Navigate to the Execution Flow Template
    • Once a valid JSON is uploaded, the modules will be displayed with their parameters and values in display mode.

In the Grounding subsection, add the required data repository types (vector, help.sap.com)

To restrict your grounding module output during inference, maintain Search Configuration value. This can help to retrieve only relevant data instead of all the uploaded data.

The Input Translation module allows you to translate LLM text prompts and grounding module output into a target language. It may help improve LLM response when the configured model performs better with input in specific language, example English.

The Data Masking module enables the anonymization or pseudonymization of data before it is sent to the LLM model for processing.

The input filters defined in the Execution Flow Template JSON are populated and displayed in the table.

The Output Filtering module allows you to filter the harmful or hateful content generated by the LLM. 

The Output Translation module allows you to translate LLM response into a target language. It helps to display the LLM response in the language user logged, if the translation is supported.

3.4) Add Prompt Template

 

 Add the grounding template

Add a Grounding Template. The grounding template can include dynamic parameters similar to the user prompt.

Add Prompt Template | SAP Help Portal

The Grounding Template retrieves relevant information from the selected data repository and appends it to the user prompt. It supports dynamic parameters, which are provided at runtime (during inference).

Add user prompt

If you have configured grounding module, at least one User Prompt must be added with the following parameter: {ISLM_GROUNDING_OUTPUT}

At runtime, this placeholder is automatically replaced with the content retrieved by the Grounding Template from the configured data repository.

add-prompt-template| SAP Help Portal 

3.6) Upload grounding documents and publish Intelligent scenario

 

Upload documents to an Intelligent Scenario, refer to the following link: SAP Help Portal | Upload Grounding Document

Below are sample return policy documents for Company Alpha and Company Beta that you can use for this example use case.

You can copy the content into separate .txt files (for example: company_alpha_return_policy.txt and company_beta_return_policy.txt) and upload them to your orchestration workflow for document grounding.

Company Alpha Return Policy

**Eligibility:**
- Items must be purchased from Alpha.com, Alpha App, or Alpha stores.
- Items must be in original condition, unworn and unwashed.
- Alpha By You custom products are eligible for return within the return window.

**Time Frame:**
- Standard items: 60 days from purchase date.
- Holiday purchases may have extended return windows.

**Condition of Items:**
- Items must include original packaging and accessories.
- Apparel must have tags attached.

**Refund Methods:**
- Refunds issued to original payment method.
- PayPal in-store purchases refunded via Alpha gift card.
- Refunds typically processed within 10 days of receipt.

**Exceptions:**
- Items from Alpha Clearance stores are not eligible.
- Items purchased from other retailers cannot be returned to Alpha.

**Return Process:**
1. Initiate return via Alpha account order history.
2. Print prepaid UPS label or use QR code.
3. Drop off at UPS or schedule pickup.
4. Refund issued after inspection.

   -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Company Beta Return Policy

**Eligibility:**
- Items must be purchased from us.Beta.com or Beta retail stores.
- Custom products are nonrefundable unless defective.
- Gift cards and bodywear including face masks are not eligible for return.

**Time Frame:**
- Standard items: 45 days from the date of purchase.
- MB.01/MB.02 Basketball Shoes: 15 days.


**Condition of Items:**
- Items must be unused and in original packaging.
- Include original form of payment and order confirmation for store returns.

**Refund Methods:**
- Refunds are issued to the original payment method.
- Beta Gift Card refunds are issued as separate E-Gift Cards.
- Refunds may take 1–2 weeks to process.

**Exceptions:**
- No returns on gift cards, customized products, and bodywear.
- No free returns for APO/FPO addresses and US Territories.

**Return Process:**
1. Initiate return via Beta returns portal.
2. Use pre-paid shipping label provided.
3. Drop off at designated location.
4. Refund processed upon receipt and inspection.

4)Deploy & Activate

Once the Intelligent Scenario is created, we can perform operations such as deployment and activation using Intelligent Scenario Management App.

Click Deploy.

You can now activate the deployment to consume the inference in your business application. You can activate deployment for your user or all users by selecting the For Me and For All options respectively.

5)Consumption of Intelligent Scenario with ABAP AI SDK

Leverage the Intelligent Scenario, together with the defined orchestration workflow, to drive automated decision-making within your business processes.

The ABAP AI SDK provides dedicated APIs to seamlessly consume inference and prompt execution results directly within your ABAP application logic.

Prompt Execution with Orchestration Service:
Use the intelligent scenario within the orchestration to drive decision-making and automation.  Once the selected deployment is activated, you can use ABAP AI SDK for inference consumption. For reference, see Utilization Of The Orchestration Service Of The Generative AI Hub.

Sample Code

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*& Report Z_TEST_ISLM_REPORT
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
REPORT Z_TEST_ISLM_REPORT.

PARAMETERS: company TYPE string OBLIGATORY.

CONSTANTS lv_scenario TYPE islm_de_sbs_is_name VALUE 'Z_ORCHESTRATION_SCEN'.

TRY.

    " Create an instance of the ABAP GenAI SDK based on an ISLM Scenario
    FINAL(api) = cl_aic_islm_orch_api_factory=>get( )->create_instance( lv_scenario ).

    api->configure_grounding( )->add_grounding_template( template_id = 'GROUNDING_TEMPLATE'
                                                         template_parameters = VALUE #( ( name = 'ISLM_GROUNDING_QUERY'
                                                                                          value = company ) ) ).

    api->configure_templating( )->add_prompt_template( message_type = aic_prompt_message_type=>user_message
                                                       template_id  = 'SUMMARIZE_RETURN_POLICY' ).

    api->set_input_parameter( name  = 'ISLM_COMPANY_NAME'
                              value = company ).

    " execute
    FINAL(response) = api->execute( ).

    " get the completion
    FINAL(answer) = response->orchestration_result( )->completion( ).

    DATA(lv_status) = 'S'.

    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = answer ).

  CATCH cx_aic_api_factory INTO DATA(lx_factory).
    DATA(error) = lx_factory->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error ).
  CATCH cx_aic_completion_api INTO DATA(lx_completion).
    DATA(error2) = lx_completion->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error2 ).
  CATCH cx_aic_prompt_template INTO DATA(lx_prompt_template).
    DATA(error3) = lx_prompt_template->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error3 ).
  CATCH cx_aic_orchestration_api INTO DATA(lx_orchestration).
    DATA(error4) = lx_orchestration->get_text( ).
    lv_status = 'E'.
    cl_demo_output=>display_text( text = error4 ).
ENDTRY.

Run the sample report with Company = COMPANY AlPHA

The output is a summary generated by large language models (LLMs).

Support for new models offered by Generative AI Hub

With the ABAP AI SDK, powered by ISLM, support has been extended to large language models (LLMs), including the following:

gpt-5
gpt-5-mini
gpt-5-nano
o3
o4-mini
gemini-2.5-flash
gemini-2.5-pro
amazon--nova-pro
amazon--nova-lite
amazon--nova-micro
mistralai--mistral-medium-instruct
mistralai--mistral-small-instruct

This enhancement broadens model support, enabling alignment with varied enterprise requirements. For detailed information on supported models, availability, please refer to the relevant SAP Note 3668194 .

Developing Your Own AI-Enabled Applications in SAP ABAP Cloud with ISLM and ABAP AI SDK 

By leveraging ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM, you can enrich your custom ABAP applications with seamlessly integrated Generative AI capabilities. This enables you to embed intelligent, context-aware features directly into your business processes while maintaining governance and clean core principles. This approach enables customers to extend SAP applications in a future-proof and upgrade-safe manner. 

Together, ISLM and ABAP AI SDK offer a powerful yet governed framework for embedding Generative AI into ABAP applications, ensuring enterprise-level reliability, security, and scalability. 

For detailed  guidance and  steps, refer to the SAP Help Portal | Developing Your Own AI-Enabled Applications: 

To get started, refer to the SAP Help Portal  .

Conclusion

Generative AI delivers real enterprise value when it is embedded directly into business processes with the right governance, scalability, and control. Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management  provides exactly that foundation, enabling structured creation, orchestration, deployment, and lifecycle management of AI scenarios within SAP S/4HANA.

With advanced runtime capabilities such as Document Grounding, Content Filtering, Data Masking, multilingual support,  reusable connectivity, and expanded model support, ISLM transforms Generative AI from experimentation into operational reality. Get started today and take your Generative AI innovation to the next level with ISLM!!

entry.title: Knowledge Graphs on SAP HANA: From Zero to Enterprise RAG with Triple Store
entry.content:
Introduction

If you’ve ever worked with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, you know the drill, you embed your documents into vectors, store them in a vector database, and at query time, you retrieve the top-K most similar chunks to feed as context to an LLM. It works. But there’s a problem.

Vector search alone is semantically “flat.” It tells you which chunks look similar to a query, but it doesn’t understand the relationships between entities in your data. It cannot reason about connections, follow chains of facts, or understand how entity A relates to entity B through entity C.

This is where Knowledge Graphs come in.

In this blog, I’ll walk you through — from absolute scratch — how i built a Knowledge Graph layer on SAP HANA using a custom Triple Store, and how i combined it with vector embedding-based retrieval to create a Hybrid RAG system that gives our AI agents both relevance (from vectors) and reasoning (from graphs).

Everything you see is from POC codebase. Let’s begin.

Table of Contents

  1. What is a Knowledge Graph?
  2. What are RDF Triplets?
  3. Why Do We Need Knowledge Graphs in RAG?
  4. Extracting Triplets from Text
    • 4a. Rule-Based Extraction with spaCy
    • 4b. LLM-Based Extraction
  5. The Multi-Agent Triplet Processing Pipeline
  6. Storing Triplets in SAP HANA Triple Store
  7. The Complete Ingestion Pipeline
  8. Querying the Knowledge Graph
  9. Hybrid Retrieval: Vector + Graph
  10. The Full RAG Pipeline
  11. Exposing via MCP for AI Agents
  12. Architecture Summary
  13. Conclusion

1. What is a Knowledge Graph?

A Knowledge Graph is a structured representation of real-world entities and the relationships between them. Think of it as a web of connected facts.

For example, consider this sentence:

“SAP SE is headquartered in Walldorf, Germany and develops enterprise software.”

A human reading this immediately understands three facts: - SAP SE → is_headquartered_in → Walldorf, Germany - SAP SE → develops → enterprise software - Walldorf → is_located_in → Germany

A Knowledge Graph captures exactly these facts as structured, queryable data. Each fact is stored as a triplet (more on this next).

Why is this different from a regular database?

Feature Relational Database Knowledge Graph

StructureFixed schema (tables, columns)Flexible (entities + relationships)
RelationshipsJOINs across tablesFirst-class citizens (edges)
Schema changesALTER TABLE neededJust add new triplets
Query styleSQL with JOINsGraph traversal / pattern matching
Best forStructured, predictable dataConnected, evolving knowledge

A Knowledge Graph is schema-flexible — you don’t need to define all possible relationships upfront. You can keep adding new types of facts without changing any table structure.

2. What are RDF Triplets?

RDF stands for Resource Description Framework. It’s a standard model for representing knowledge as triplets — the atomic unit of a Knowledge Graph.

Every triplet has three parts:

(Subject, Predicate, Object)
  • Subject: The entity we’re talking about (e.g., “SAP SE”)
  • Predicate: The relationship or property (e.g., “is_headquartered_in”)
  • Object: The value or target entity (e.g., “Walldorf”)

Visual Representation

    [SAP SE] ---is_headquartered_in--→ [Walldorf]
       |                                    |
       |---develops--→ [Enterprise Software] |
                                            |
                       [Germany] ←--is_located_in---┘

Each arrow is a triplet. A collection of triplets forms a graph — hence “Knowledge Graph.”

Why Triplets?

Triplets are powerful because they are: 1. Atomic — Each triplet is a self-contained fact 2. Composable — You can combine millions of triplets into a unified graph 3. Queryable — You can traverse relationships: “What does SAP SE develop?” or “Where is the company headquartered?” 4. Language-agnostic — The structure is universal regardless of source language

3. Why Do We Need Knowledge Graphs in RAG?

Let’s understand this with a concrete example.

The Problem with Vector-Only RAG

Imagine your enterprise has documents about employees, projects, and departments. A user asks:

“Who is the project lead for Project Phoenix and which department does that person belong to?”

With vector-only RAG, the system: 1. Embeds the query into a vector 2. Finds the top-K most similar document chunks 3. Hopes that one single chunk contains all the information

But what if: - Chunk A says: “John Smith is the project lead for Project Phoenix” - Chunk B says: “John Smith works in the Cloud Engineering department”

Vector search might retrieve Chunk A (it’s most similar to the query), but miss Chunk B entirely. The LLM then cannot answer the full question.

How Knowledge Graphs Solve This

With a Knowledge Graph, we have these triplets stored:

(John Smith, is_project_lead_of, Project Phoenix)
(John Smith, works_in, Cloud Engineering)
(Cloud Engineering, is_department_of, SAP)

When the system retrieves Chunk A via vector search and finds “John Smith,” it can traverse the graph to discover that John Smith works in Cloud Engineering — even though that second fact was in a completely different document chunk.

The Power of Hybrid Retrieval

Capability Vector Search Knowledge Graph Hybrid (Both)

Semantic similarity✅❌✅
Relationship traversal❌✅✅
Multi-hop reasoning❌✅✅
Handles unseen phrasing✅❌✅
Entity connections❌✅✅

By combining both, we get the best of both worlds: semantic understanding from vectors AND structured reasoning from graphs.

4. Extracting Triplets from Text

The first step in building a Knowledge Graph is extracting triplets from unstructured text. i implemented two approaches in our system.

4a. Rule-Based Extraction with spaCy (NLP Approach)

Our first approach uses spaCy, a natural language processing library, to extract Subject-Verb-Object (SVO) patterns from sentences.

Here’s the code from our triplets_service.py:

import re
from typing import List, Tuple
import spacy

_nlp = None

def get_nlp():
    global _nlp
    if _nlp is None:
        _nlp = spacy.load("en_core_web_sm")
    return _nlp

def clean(text: str) -> str:
    """Basic text cleaning function"""
    return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", text).strip()

def extract_svo_spacy(sentence: str):
    nlp = get_nlp()
    doc = nlp(sentence)
    triplets = []
    for token in doc:
        if token.pos_ == "VERB":  # processing every verb
            # subject can be to the left or as a child
            subj = [w for w in token.children if w.dep_ in ("nsubj", "nsubjpass")]
            objs = [w for w in token.children if w.dep_ in ("dobj", "attr", "dative", "oprd")]

            # prepositional objects
            for prep in [w for w in token.children if w.dep_ == "prep"]:
                pobj = [w for w in prep.children if w.dep_ == "pobj"]
                objs.extend(pobj)

            if subj and objs:
                for o in objs:
                    triplets.append((clean(subj[0].text), clean(token.lemma_), clean(o.text)))
    return triplets

 

How This Works — Step by Step

  1. Load the spaCy model (en_core_web_sm) — a pre-trained English NLP model
  2. Parse the sentence — spaCy tokenizes it and identifies parts of speech (POS) and dependency relations
  3. Find every VERB — verbs are potential predicates in our triplets
  4. Find subjects — tokens with dependency labels nsubj (nominal subject) or nsubjpass (passive nominal subject)
  5. Find objects — tokens with dependency labels dobj (direct object), attr (attribute), etc.
  6. Handle prepositional objects — “works in engineering” → the object is “engineering” via the preposition “in”
  7. Build triplets — (subject_text, verb_lemma, object_text)

Example:
Input: "SAP develops enterprise software"
Output: [("SAP", "develop", "software")]

Batch Processing

i also have a batch function in triplets_service.py to process an entire corpus:

def extract_corpus_triplets(corpus: List[str]) -> List[List[Tuple[str, str, str]]]:
    """extracting triplets from a list of text chunks"""
    results = []
    for text in corpus:
        sent = text.strip()
        if not sent:
            continue
        triplets = extract_svo_spacy(sent)
        results.append(triplets)
    return results

Limitations of Rule-Based Extraction

While fast and deterministic, spaCy-based extraction has limitations: - Misses implicit relationships (“Headquartered in Walldorf since 1972” — the subject “SAP” isn’t in this fragment) - Produces shallow predicates (just the verb lemma, not semantic relationship names) - Struggles with complex sentences and nested clauses

This is why i built a second, more powerful approach.

4b. LLM-Based Extraction (AI-Powered Approach)

Our primary extraction method uses a Large Language Model (via SAP AI Core) to understand text semantically and produce high-quality triplets.

Here’s the code from our knowledge_graph_service.py:

async def generate_triplets(text_chunk: str):
    """
    Uses an LLM via the Generative AI Hub SDK to extract RDF triplets
    from a given text chunk.
    """
    prompt_for_triplets = f"""
    Extract key factual statements from the text as RDF triplets.

    STRICT REQUIREMENTS:
    1. Return ONLY a valid JSON object
    2. Use exactly this format: {{"triplets": [["subject", "predicate", "object"]]}}
    3. Each triplet must have exactly 3 elements
    4. Use clear, concise predicates (e.g., "is", "has", "uses", "located_in")
    5. Maximum 15 triplets per response
    6. No explanations, no extra text outside JSON

    Text: "{text_chunk}"

    JSON:
    """

    try:
        response_content = await get_llm_response_async(prompt=prompt_for_triplets)
        response_json = json.loads(response_content)
        triplets = response_json.get("triplets", [])
        # converting the inner lists to tuples for consistency
        return [tuple(triplet) for triplet in triplets]

    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        print("Failed to parse JSON response from LLM.")
        return []
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"An error occurred: {e}")
        return []

 

How This Works

  1. Craft a precise prompt — We instruct the LLM to extract facts as [subject, predicate, object] arrays in JSON format
  2. Call the LLM asynchronously — via get_llm_response_async() which hits SAP AI Core’s inference API
  3. Parse the JSON response — extract the triplets array
  4. Convert to tuples — for consistency with the rest of our pipeline
  5. Handle errors gracefully — JSON parse failures and API errors return empty lists

Why LLM-Based Extraction is Superior

Aspect spaCy (Rule-Based) LLM (AI-Powered)

Implicit relationships❌Misses them✅Infers from context
Semantic predicates❌Raw verb lemmas✅Meaningful labels like “is_headquartered_in”
Complex sentences❌Often fails✅Handles well
Consistency✅Deterministic⚠️May vary (hence we validate)
Speed✅Very fast⚠️API call overhead
Cost✅Free⚠️LLM API costs

Batch Processing with Async Concurrency

Processing an entire corpus (potentially hundreds of chunks) sequentially would be extremely slow. i use asyncio.gather for concurrent processing:

async def convert_corpus_to_triplets_async(
    corpus: list,
    use_orchestrator: bool = True,
) -> List[List[Tuple[str, str, str]]]:
    """Convert entire corpus to triplets using async concurrency"""
    if not corpus:
        return []

    try:
        if use_orchestrator:
            orchestrator = TripletOrchestrator()
            results = await orchestrator.process_corpus(corpus)
            return results
        else:
            # Run all LLM calls concurrently
            tasks = [generate_triplets(text) for text in corpus]
            results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)

            processed_results = []
            for i, result in enumerate(results):
                if isinstance(result, Exception):
                    print(f"Error processing chunk {i}: {result}")
                    processed_results.append([])
                else:
                    processed_results.append(result)
            return processed_results

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error in convert_corpus_to_triplets_async: {e}")
        return [[]] * len(corpus)

 

Notice the use_orchestrator flag — when True, i use our Multi-Agent Pipeline for higher quality triplets. Let’s dive into that next.

5. The Multi-Agent Triplet Processing Pipeline

Raw LLM output isn’t always perfect. The model might produce inconsistent predicate names, duplicate triplets, or factually questionable relationships. To solve this, i built a multi-agent processing pipeline where specialized agents collaborate to refine triplets.

Architecture Overview

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   TripletOrchestrator                     │
│                                                          │
│  ┌────────────┐   ┌───────────┐   ┌───────────┐        │
│  │  Analyzer   │──→│  Semantic  │──→│ Validator  │        │
│  │   Agent     │   │  Cleaner   │   │   Agent    │        │
│  └────────────┘   └───────────┘   └───────────┘        │
│        │                                    │            │
│        │          ┌───────────┐              │            │
│        └─error──→ │   JSON     │              │            │
│                   │  Repair    │              │            │
│                   └───────────┘              │            │
│                                    ┌────────▼────────┐  │
│                                    │   Aggregator     │  │
│                                    │     Agent        │  │
│                                    └─────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The State Object

All agents share a common state object defined in state_schema.py:

class TripletState(BaseModel):
    """State management for triplet processing pipeline"""
    raw_text: str
    initial_triplets: List[List[str]] = []
    cleaned_triplets: List[List[str]] = []
    validated_triplets: List[List[str]] = []
    final_triplets: List[List[str]] = []

    # metadata and tracking
    processing_state: str = "initial"
    error_messages: List[str] = []
    quality_scores: Dict[str, float] = {}
    retry_count: int = 0
    max_retries: int = 3

    # agent outputs
    analyzer_feedback: Optional[str] = None
    cleaner_feedback: Optional[str] = None
    validator_feedback: Optional[str] = None

 

This is a shared state pattern — each agent reads from and writes to specific fields, and the orchestrator controls the flow.

Stage 1: Analyzer Agent

The AnalyzerAgent in analyzer_node.py performs the initial extraction with a quality assessment:

class AnalyzerAgent:
    """Analyzes text and extracts initial triplets with quality assessment"""

    async def process(self, state: TripletState) -> TripletState:
        try:
            prompt = self._create_analysis_prompt(state.raw_text)
            response = await get_llm_response_async(prompt)
            analysis_result = self._parse_response(response)

            if analysis_result["success"]:
                state.initial_triplets = analysis_result["triplets"]
                state.quality_scores["analyzer"] = analysis_result["quality_score"]
                state.analyzer_feedback = analysis_result["feedback"]
                state.processing_state = "analyzed"
            else:
                state.error_messages.append(f"Analyzer failed: {analysis_result['error']}")
        except Exception as e:
            state.error_messages.append(f"Analyzer exception: {str(e)}")
        return state

The analyzer’s prompt asks the LLM to not only extract triplets but also rate the quality (0-1 score) and provide feedback about text complexity. This metadata helps downstream agents.

Stage 2: Semantic Cleaner Agent

The SemanticCleanerAgent in semantic_cleaner_agent.py standardizes the triplets:

class SemanticCleanerAgent:
    """Cleans and standardizes triplets for consistency"""

    def _create_cleaning_prompt(self, triplets: List[List[str]]) -> str:
        triplets_str = json.dumps(triplets)
        return f"""
        Clean and standardize the following triplets for better semantic consistency.

        CLEANING TASKS:
        1. Normalize entity names (remove extra spaces, fix capitalization)
        2. Standardize predicates (use consistent naming like "is_a", "has_property", "located_in")
        3. Remove duplicate or very similar triplets
        4. Fix obvious semantic issues
        5. Ensure subjects and objects are meaningful entities

        REQUIREMENTS:
        - Maintain factual accuracy
        - Use snake_case for predicates
        - Remove triplets with generic or meaningless entities

        Original triplets: {triplets_str}
        """

 

This agent ensures that predicates like "is located in", "located_in", and "is_in" all become a consistent "located_in". This consistency is critical for reliable graph queries later.

Stage 3: Validator Agent

The TripletValidatorAgent in triplet_validator_agent.py cross-checks triplets against the original text:

class TripletValidatorAgent:
    """Validates triplets for factual accuracy and relevance"""

    def _create_validation_prompt(self, triplets, original_text):
        return f"""
        Validate the following triplets against the original text for factual accuracy.

        VALIDATION CRITERIA:
        1. Factual accuracy: Are the relationships stated correctly?
        2. Semantic validity: Do the predicates make sense?
        3. Completeness: Are important facts missing?
        4. Consistency: Are there contradictions?
        5. Relevance: Are all triplets relevant to the source text?

        Original text: "{original_text}"
        Triplets to validate: {json.dumps(triplets)}
        """

 

This is a crucial fact-checking step — the validator has access to both the triplets AND the original text, so it can verify that no hallucinated facts slipped through.

Stage 4: Aggregator Agent

The AggregatorAgent in aggregator_node.py selects the best output and calculates an overall quality score:

class AggregatorAgent:
    """Aggregates results from all agents and determines final output"""

    def _select_best_triplets(self, state: TripletState) -> List[List[str]]:
        """Select the best quality triplets from processing pipeline"""
        if state.validated_triplets:
            return state.validated_triplets
        elif state.cleaned_triplets:
            return state.cleaned_triplets
        elif state.initial_triplets:
            return state.initial_triplets
        else:
            return []

    def _calculate_overall_quality(self, state: TripletState) -> float:
        """Weighted average quality score"""
        weights = {
            "analyzer": 0.3,
            "cleaner": 0.3,
            "validator": 0.4   # validator has higher weight
        }
        weighted_sum = 0.0
        total_weight = 0.0
        for agent, weight in weights.items():
            if agent in state.quality_scores:
                weighted_sum += state.quality_scores[agent] * weight
                total_weight += weight
        return weighted_sum / total_weight if total_weight > 0 else 0.0

 

Notice the graceful degradation — if validation fails, it falls back to cleaned triplets; if cleaning fails, it uses the initial triplets. The system never completely fails.

The Orchestrator

The TripletOrchestrator in orchestrator.py wires everything together with retry logic:

class TripletOrchestrator:
    def __init__(self):
        self.analyzer = AnalyzerAgent()
        self.cleaner = SemanticCleanerAgent()
        self.validator = TripletValidatorAgent()
        self.aggregator = AggregatorAgent()
        self.json_repair = JSONRepairAgent()

    async def preprocess_text_chunk(self, text_chunk: str) -> List[Tuple[str, str, str]]:
        state = TripletState(raw_text=text_chunk)
        try:
            # Stage 1: Initial analysis (with retries)
            while True:
                state = await self.analyzer.process(state)
                if not self._should_retry(state):
                    break
                state.retry_count += 1

            # Stage 2: Semantic cleaning
            if state.initial_triplets and not self._has_critical_errors(state):
                while True:
                    state = await self.cleaner.process(state)
                    if not self._should_retry(state):
                        break
                    state.retry_count += 1

            # Stage 3: Validation
            if (state.cleaned_triplets or state.initial_triplets) and not self._has_critical_errors(state):
                while True:
                    state = await self.validator.process(state)
                    if not self._should_retry(state):
                        break
                    state.retry_count += 1

            # Stage 4: Aggregation (no retries needed)
            state = self.aggregator.process(state)
            return [tuple(triplet) for triplet in state.final_triplets]

        except Exception as e:
            return []

 

Each stage has retry logic — if an agent fails (e.g., LLM returns malformed JSON), the orchestrator retries up to max_retries (3) times before moving to the next stage.

6. Storing Triplets in SAP HANA Triple Store

Now that we can extract high-quality triplets, we need somewhere to store them. We built a Triple Store as a column table in SAP HANA.

Table Schema

From our hana_repository.py, here’s the table creation:

def ensure_triple_store():
    """Create TRIPLE STORE table and indexes if they don't exist"""
    conn = get_hana_db()
    try:
        # Check if table exists
        exists_sql = """
        SELECT 1 FROM SYS.TABLES
        WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'TRIPLE_STORE'
        AND SCHEMA_NAME = CURRENT_SCHEMA
        """
        result = conn.sql(exists_sql).collect()
        if not result.empty:
            return

        # Create the table
        create_sql = """
        CREATE COLUMN TABLE TRIPLE_STORE (
            ID BIGINT GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
            EMB_REF_ID NVARCHAR(36) NOT NULL,
            CHUNK_INDEX INTEGER,
            SUBJECT NVARCHAR(500),
            PREDICATE NVARCHAR(200),
            OBJECT NVARCHAR(1000),
            CREATED_AT TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_UTCTIMESTAMP,
            FOREIGN KEY (EMB_REF_ID) REFERENCES DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING(ref_id)
        )
        """
        conn.execute_sql(create_sql)

        # Create indexes for fast lookups
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_SUBJ ON TRIPLE_STORE (SUBJECT)")
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_PRED ON TRIPLE_STORE (PREDICATE)")
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_OBJ ON TRIPLE_STORE (OBJECT)")
        conn.execute_sql("CREATE INDEX IDX_TRIPLE_REF ON TRIPLE_STORE (EMB_REF_ID, CHUNK_INDEX)")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"error ensuring triple store: {e}")

 

Understanding the Schema

Column Type Purpose

IDBIGINT (auto)Unique triplet identifier
EMB_REF_IDNVARCHAR(36)Links to the embedding table — this is the bridge between vectors and graph
CHUNK_INDEXINTEGERWhich chunk within a document this triplet came from
SUBJECTNVARCHAR(500)The subject entity
PREDICATENVARCHAR(200)The relationship
OBJECTNVARCHAR(1000)The object entity
CREATED_ATTIMESTAMPWhen the triplet was created

The EMB_REF_ID foreign key is the critical design decision. It links every triplet back to its source embedding in the DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING table. This means: - Given a vector search result, we can find its associated triplets - Given a triplet, we can find the original text chunk - The two systems (vector + graph) are tightly integrated

Indexes for Performance

i created four indexes for fast querying: - IDX_TRIPLE_SUBJ — look up triplets by subject - IDX_TRIPLE_PRED — look up triplets by predicate - IDX_TRIPLE_OBJ — look up triplets by object - IDX_TRIPLE_REF — look up triplets by source embedding (composite index)

The Embedding Table (for reference)

The companion DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING table stores the vector embeddings:

CREATE COLUMN TABLE DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING (
    id INTEGER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY,
    document_text NVARCHAR(5000),
    embedding REAL_VECTOR(3072),
    chunk_metadata NVARCHAR(1000),
    ref_id NVARCHAR(36) UNIQUE NOT NULL
)

 

REAL_VECTOR(3072) is SAP HANA’s native vector type — it stores 3072-dimensional embeddings (from OpenAI’s text-embedding-3-large model) and supports cosine similarity searches natively.

Inserting Triplets

def insert_triplets(triplets_rows: list):
    """
    Insert triplets with their metadata
    triplets_rows: list of (ref_id, chunk_index, subject, predicate, object) tuples
    """
    ensure_triple_store()
    conn = get_hana_db()

    if not triplets_rows:
        return False

    sql = """
        INSERT INTO TRIPLE_STORE (EMB_REF_ID, CHUNK_INDEX, SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT)
        VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    """
    try:
        cur = conn.connection.cursor()
        cur.executemany(sql, triplets_rows)
        conn.connection.commit()
        cur.close()
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        return False

 

We use executemany for batch insertion — inserting hundreds of triplets in a single database round-trip instead of one-by-one.

7. The Complete Ingestion Pipeline

Let’s see how everything comes together in our document_processing_service.py. This is the end-to-end flow: from a raw document URL to stored embeddings AND triplets.

async def process_and_embed_file_from_url(file_url: str):
    """
    Download file, extract text, create embeddings and triplets,
    store everything in HANA
    """
    # Step 1: Download and extract text
    text_content = process_file_from_url(file_url)

    # Step 2: Split into overlapping chunks
    chunks = split_text_into_chunks(text_content)

    # Step 3: Preprocess chunks
    preprocessed_chunks = preprocess_text_chunks(chunks)

    # Step 4: Generate unique reference IDs (linking embeddings ↔ triplets)
    ref_ids = [str(uuid.uuid4()) for _ in preprocessed_chunks]

    # Step 5: Create embeddings for all chunks (concurrent)
    embeddings = await embedding_service.get_embeddings_batch(preprocessed_chunks, max_workers=5)

    # Step 6: Extract triplets from all chunks (multi-agent pipeline)
    triplets_per_chunk = await convert_corpus_to_triplets_async(preprocessed_chunks)

    # Step 7: Insert embeddings FIRST (ref_ids must exist for FK constraint)
    rows = []
    for i, (chunk, embedding_vector) in enumerate(zip(preprocessed_chunks, embeddings)):
        metadata_json = json.dumps({
            "chunk_index": i,
            "chunk_size": len(chunk),
            "source_url": file_url,
            "ref_id": ref_ids[i],
        })
        embedding_string = str(embedding_vector)
        rows.append((chunk, embedding_string, metadata_json, ref_ids[i]))

    batch_insertion_embedding(rows=rows)

    # Step 8: Insert triplets (linked via ref_ids)
    triplets_rows = []
    for chunk_index, (ref_id, chunk_triplets) in enumerate(zip(ref_ids, triplets_per_chunk)):
        for t in chunk_triplets:
            if isinstance(t, dict):
                head, relation, tail = t.get("subject"), t.get("predicate"), t.get("object")
            else:
                head, relation, tail = t
            triplets_rows.append((ref_id, chunk_index, head, relation, tail))

    if triplets_rows:
        insert_triplets(triplets_rows)

 

The Text Chunking Strategy

def split_text_into_chunks(text: str, chunk_size: int = 1000, overlap: int = 200):
    """Split text into overlapping chunks for better embedding"""
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(text):
        end = start + chunk_size
        chunk = text[start:end]
        chunks.append(chunk)
        start = end - overlap
    return chunks

 

i used overlapping chunks (200 characters overlap) so that facts spanning chunk boundaries aren’t lost. Each chunk is 1000 characters with a 200-character overlap with the next chunk.

The API Endpoint

This entire pipeline is exposed via a FastAPI endpoint in genai_hub.py:

_router.post("/create-store-embedding", response_model=EmbeddingKGResponse)
async def create_and_store_embedding(
    request: EmbeddingKGRequest,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks
) -> EmbeddingKGResponse:
    """Create and store embeddings using file URL"""
    # Processing in background (non-blocking)
    background_tasks.add_task(process_and_embed_file_from_url, request.file_url)

    return EmbeddingKGResponse(
        success=True,
        message=f"Successfully processed and embedded document from {request.file_url}"
    )

 

The processing runs as a background task — the API responds immediately while the heavy work (embedding + triplet extraction) happens asynchronously.

8. Querying the Knowledge Graph

Now for the exciting part — retrieving knowledge from our graph. i have two query strategies in knowledge_graph_service.py.

Strategy 1: Chunk-Based Retrieval

Given a set of embedding reference IDs (from vector search results), fetch all associated triplets:

def get_triplets_by_chunks(ref_ids: list, query: str):
    """Getting triplets from specific chunks + optional query expansion"""
    conn = get_hana_db()

    ref_placeholders = ",".join(f"'{ref}'" for ref in ref_ids)
    base_sql = f"""
    SELECT SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT
    FROM TRIPLE_STORE
    WHERE EMB_REF_ID IN ({ref_placeholders})
    ORDER BY CHUNK_INDEX, ID
    LIMIT 200
    """

    triplets = []
    try:
        df = conn.sql(base_sql).collect()
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            triplets.append((row["SUBJECT"], row["PREDICATE"], row["OBJECT"]))
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"An error occurred: {e}")

    # Optional: expand with query-related triplets
    if query and triplets:
        expanded = search_related_triplets(
            query, existing_entities=[t[0] for t in triplets[:10]]
        )
        triplets.extend(expanded)

    return triplets

 

This function does two things: 1. Direct retrieval — gets all triplets linked to the vector search results 2. Graph expansion — discovers additional related triplets based on entities found

Strategy 2: Keyword + Entity Expansion

The search_related_triplets function implements a two-phase search:

def search_related_triplets(query: str, existing_entities: list, limit: int = 50):
    """Searching triplets by keyword + entity expansion"""
    conn = get_hana_db()
    triplets = []

    # Phase 1: Keyword search in triplets
    query_clean = query.replace("'", "''")
    keyword_sql = f"""
    SELECT SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT
    FROM TRIPLE_STORE
    WHERE SUBJECT LIKE '%{query_clean}%'
    OR OBJECT LIKE '%{query_clean}%'
    OR PREDICATE LIKE '%{query_clean}%'
    LIMIT {limit}
    """
    try:
        df = conn.sql(keyword_sql).collect()
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            triplets.append((row["SUBJECT"], row["PREDICATE"], row["OBJECT"]))
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"error in keyword triplet search: {e}")

    # Phase 2: Entity expansion
    if existing_entities:
        entities_clean = [e.replace("'", "''") for e in existing_entities[:5]]
        entities_placeholders = "','".join(entities_clean)
        expansion_sql = f"""
        SELECT SUBJECT, PREDICATE, OBJECT
        FROM TRIPLE_STORE
        WHERE SUBJECT IN ('{entities_placeholders}')
        OR OBJECT IN ('{entities_placeholders}')
        LIMIT {limit}
        """
        try:
            df = conn.sql(expansion_sql).collect()
            for _, row in df.iterrows():
                triplets.append((row["SUBJECT"], row["PREDICATE"], row["OBJECT"]))
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"error in entity expansion triplet search: {e}")

    return triplets

 

Phase 1 (Keyword Search): Finds triplets where the query terms appear anywhere in subject, predicate, or object. This catches direct mentions.

Phase 2 (Entity Expansion): Takes entities discovered in Phase 1 and finds ALL triplets mentioning those entities. This is the “graph traversal” — following connections one hop outward.

This is how we achieve multi-hop reasoning: Vector search finds relevant chunks → chunk triplets reveal entities → entity expansion reveals connected facts from other chunks.

9. Hybrid Retrieval: Vector + Graph

The ContextService in context_service.py is where vector search and knowledge graph come together:

class ContextService:
    """Context service with cache for RAG Pipeline"""

    async def hybrid_search_context(self, query: str, top_k: int = 5, expand_graph: bool = True) -> str:
        """
        Hybrid retrieval: vector similarity + graph retrieval
        Returns combined textual context for RAG
        """
        # Step 1: Generate query embedding
        query_embedding = await embedding_service.get_embedding(query)

        # Step 2: Vector similarity search
        vector_results = await search_similiar_documents(query_embedding, top_k=top_k)

        if vector_results is None or vector_results.empty:
            return ""

        context_parts = []
        ref_ids = []

        # Step 3: Extract results and collect ref_ids
        for _, row in vector_results.iterrows():
            ref_id = row.get("REF_ID") or row.get("ref_id")
            chunk_text = row.get("DOCUMENT_TEXT", '')
            similarity = row.get("SIMILARITY", 0)

            context_parts.append(f"Chunk (similarity: {similarity:.4f}): {chunk_text}")
            if ref_id:
                ref_ids.append(ref_id)

        # Step 4: Graph expansion using ref_ids from vector results
        if expand_graph and ref_ids:
            graph_context = get_triplets_by_chunks(ref_ids, query)
            if graph_context:
                context_parts.append(f"\nRelated Facts: {graph_context}")

        return "\n\n".join(context_parts)

 

The Hybrid Flow Visualized

User Query: "Who leads Project Phoenix?"
        │
        ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  1. Embed Query          │  → [0.023, -0.156, 0.891, ...]
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  2. Vector Search        │  → Top 5 similar chunks
│  (COSINE_SIMILARITY)     │     with ref_ids
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  3. Graph Expansion                      │
│  - Fetch triplets for those ref_ids      │
│  - Keyword search for "Project Phoenix"  │
│  - Entity expansion for found entities   │
└────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  4. Combine Context      │
│  - Chunk texts           │  → "Chunk (similarity: 0.92): ..."
│  - Related facts         │  → "Related Facts: [(John Smith, leads, Project Phoenix), ...]"
└────────────┬────────────┘
             │
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  5. Send to LLM          │  → Final answer with full context
└─────────────────────────┘

10. The Full RAG Pipeline

Everything comes together in the /chat endpoint in RAG_pipeline.py:

_pipeline_router.post("/chat", response_model=RAGChatResponse)
async def rag_chat(request: RAGChatRequest) -> RAGChatResponse:
    """Complete RAG pipeline: Retrieve + Augment + Generate"""
    try:
        # Check cache first
        cache_key = context_service.cache.make_key(
            "rag_response",
            _make_query_hash(request.query, request.temperature, request.max_tokens),
        )
        cached_response = await context_service.cache.get(cache_key)
        if cached_response:
            cached_response["from_cache"] = True
            return RAGChatResponse(**cached_response)

        # Hybrid retrieval (vector + graph)
        context = await context_service.hybrid_search_context(
            request.query, top_k=request.k, expand_graph=True
        )

        if context == "":
            return RAGChatResponse(
                success=True, query=request.query,
                answer="I couldn't find any relevant documents to answer your question.",
            )

        # Generate answer with augmented context
        answer = await generate_rag_response(
            request.query, context, request.temperature, request.max_tokens
        )

        response_data = {
            "success": True, "query": request.query,
            "answer": answer, "context_used": context, "from_cache": False
        }

        # Cache for 1 hour
        await context_service.cache.set(cache_key, response_data, ttl=3600)
        return RAGChatResponse(**response_data)
    except Exception as e:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail={"success": False, "error": str(e)})

 

The prompt sent to the LLM for generation:

async def generate_rag_response(query, context, temperature=0.1, max_tokens=500):
    prompt = f"""
    Based on the following context, answer the question:

    Context:
    {context}

    Question: {query}

    Answer based only on the context provided:
    """
    response = await llm_service.get_llm_response_async(prompt=prompt)
    return response

 

The context now contains both similar text chunks (from vectors) and structured facts (from the knowledge graph). The LLM gets the richest possible context to generate its answer.

11. Exposing via MCP for AI Agents

i also expose our hybrid RAG as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool in mcp/server.py, so AI agents can use it directly:

from fastmcp import FastMCP

mcp = FastMCP("AI-RAG-Service")

@mcp.tool()
async def rag_chat(query: str, top_k: int = 3, temperature: float = 0.1) -> str:
    """Chat with RAG pipeline - retrieves context and generates answer"""
    # Get context using hybrid search (vector + knowledge graph)
    context = await context_service.hybrid_search_context(
        query, top_k=top_k, expand_graph=True
    )
    if not context:
        return "I couldn't find any relevant documents to answer your question."

    prompt = f"""Based on the following context, answer the question:
    Context: {context}
    Question: {query}
    Answer based only on the context provided:"""

    answer = await llm_service.get_llm_response_async(prompt=prompt)
    return answer

 

This means any MCP-compatible agent (like those built with LangChain, LangGraph, or Claude) can call our rag_chat tool and get knowledge-graph-augmented answers automatically.

12. Architecture Summary

Here’s the complete system architecture:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        INGESTION PIPELINE                          │
│                                                                     │
│  Document URL → Download → Text Extraction → Chunking (1000/200)   │
│       │                                                             │
│       ├──→ Embedding Service ──→ DOCUMENTS_EMBEDDING table          │
│       │    (text-embedding-3-large, 3072 dims)                      │
│       │                                                             │
│       └──→ Multi-Agent Triplet Pipeline ──→ TRIPLE_STORE table      │
│            (Analyzer → Cleaner → Validator → Aggregator)            │
│                                                                     │
│  Tables linked by ref_id (UUID) ←─ Foreign Key relationship        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        RETRIEVAL PIPELINE                           │
│                                                                     │
│  User Query → Embed Query → Cosine Similarity Search (HANA Vector) │
│       │                                                             │
│       ├──→ Top-K similar chunks (text + ref_ids)                    │
│       │                                                             │
│       └──→ Graph Expansion (ref_id triplets + keyword + entities)   │
│                                                                     │
│  Combined Context → LLM Prompt → Generated Answer                   │
│                                                                     │
│  Exposed via: FastAPI REST + MCP Tools for AI Agents                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

13. Conclusion

Knowledge Graphs are not just an academic concept — they’re a practical, powerful enhancement to any enterprise RAG system. By combining vector embeddings with structured knowledge triplets stored in SAP HANA’s Triple Store, i achieved:

  1. Better answer quality — The LLM receives both semantically similar text AND structured facts
  2. Multi-hop reasoning — Entity expansion allows discovering connected facts across different documents
  3. Production-grade reliability — Multi-agent pipeline with retries, validation, and graceful degradation
  4. Tight integration — Foreign key linking between embeddings and triplets enables seamless hybrid retrieval
  5. Agent-ready — MCP tools let any AI agent leverage the knowledge graph instantly

Key Takeaways for Your Team

  • Start with LLM-based triplet extraction — it produces far better triplets than rule-based NLP
  • Always validate — the multi-agent pipeline (analyze → clean → validate → aggregate) catches errors early
  • Link your stores — the ref_id foreign key between embeddings and triplets is what makes hybrid retrieval possible
  • Index aggressively — Subject, Predicate, Object, and composite indexes make graph queries fast
  • Expand the graph — entity expansion is where the real magic happens, discovering connections the vector search alone would miss

The future of enterprise AI isn’t just “more data”, it’s more connected data. Knowledge Graphs give your AI agents not just context, but direction.

Have questions or want to discuss further? Reach out to me

entry.title: Unlocking Transformational Value at SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando
entry.content:
Unlocking Transformational Value at SAP Sapphire: Join the Exclusive ASUG Pre-Conference on the New SAP Services & Support Portfolio

SAP is entering a new era of services and support—one that is simpler, more intelligent, and designed to help organizations move faster toward cloud transformation and AI-driven innovation. To help customers understand and fully leverage this new approach, ASUG and SAP are hosting an exclusive full-day pre-conference at SAP Sapphire:

“Unlocking Transformational Value: A Practical Introduction to the New SAP Services & Support Portfolio.”

This immersive event brings together SAP leaders, product experts, and customers to walk through the modernized SAP Services & Support portfolio, explain the new tiered Success Plans, and demonstrate how organizations can accelerate cloud adoption, improve operations, and drive measurable business outcomes. With breakfast, lunch, expert sessions, and an exclusive showfloor tour, the day provides both strategic insight and practical guidance for anyone responsible for SAP transformation. Whether you are planning a cloud migration, optimizing hybrid landscapes, or exploring how AI can transform operations, this session will help you understand how to maximize value from your SAP investment.
Register Now!

What You’ll Learn

During this full-day experience, participants will explore:

  • The new SAP Success Plans: Foundational, Advanced, and Max

  • How AI-enabled services improve productivity and automation

  • Ways to optimize SAP operations and governance

  • How Development Services and co-innovation accelerate innovation

  • How Application Management and Sovereign Cloud support modern enterprise requirements

You’ll leave with practical guidance for building your SAP success strategy for 2026 and beyond. Register Now!

Full Agenda May 11 | Orlando

Note: Session agenda, elements, and speakers are subject to change.

8:30–8:15 AM — Welcome

Welcome & Setting the Stage

Speakers:
Geoff Scott, CEO and Chief Community Champion, ASUG
Stefan Steinle, EVP and Head of Global Customer Support, SAP SE

Kick off the day with ASUG and SAP leadership as they outline why SAP simplified its Services & Support portfolio. Learn what customers asked for and how the new model supports cloud adoption, AI-driven innovation, and predictable, business-aligned outcomes.

8:45–9:15 AM — Keynote Session

The New SAP Services & Support Portfolio: Simpler, Smarter, Predictable

Speakers:
Thomas Pfiester, Head of Customer Engagement & Adoption and Member of the Extended Board of SAP SE
Stefan Steinle, EVP and Head Global Customer Support, SAP
Uwe Grigoleit, SVP, Head of SAP Customer Evolution and Portfolio

Discover the structure and strategy behind SAP’s new Services & Support portfolio. This keynote introduces SAP Success Plans (Foundational, Advanced, and Max) alongside Development Services, Application Management, Professional Services, and SAP Sovereign Cloud. Learn how AI-enabled services simplify operations, increase predictability, and accelerate adoption, innovation, and business value.

9:15–9:25 AM — Break

9:25–9:55 AM — Success Plan Deep Dive

Foundational to get started and achieve continuous value with confidence

Speakers:
Rohan Patel, Global Head of Support, SAP Procurement
Jens Bernotat, Global Vice President Strategy, Portfolio, Global Customer Support, SAP

Learn what’s included in the SAP Foundational Success Plan, including SAP Enterprise Support, digital guidance, SAP Cloud ALM, and best practices. This session highlights how organizations can onboard faster, maintain system stability, and continuously improve operations across their SAP cloud landscape.

9:55–10:25 AM — Spotlight Session

Transforming the Cloud Journey with SAP Cloud ALM and Next-Generation Agents

Speakers:
Björn Braemer, SVP & Program Executive, Customer Evolution Program, SAP
Thorsten Spihlmann, Head of Business Development for Data Transformation, SAP

SAP Cloud ALM is evolving into a Transformation Execution Platform, helping organizations accelerate cloud transformation. Discover how an enhanced AI toolchain and agentic AI capabilities support configuration, migration, data preparation, and automated testing—reducing manual effort and enabling faster, smarter transformation journeys.

10:25–11:00 AM — Success Plan Deep Dive

Advanced for Guided, Predictable Transformation

Speakers:
Peter Roberts, SVP & Chief Product Officer, Advanced Success Plan, SAP

The Advanced Success Plan provides expert-guided engagement for complex transformation programs. Learn how tailored guidance, architectural reviews, readiness checks, and proactive insights help ensure predictable adoption and business-aligned outcomes across regions and teams.

 

11:00–11:35 PM — Success Plan Deep Dive

Max for Strategic Partnership and Business-Critical Success

Speakers:
Pallavi Kothuri, Marathon Petroleum
Sebastien Denes, Global Vice President, Chief Product Officer Max Success Plan, SAP

Explore SAP’s highest level of engagement with the Max Success Plan. Learn how dedicated experts, proactive governance, AI-driven insights, and mission-critical operations support help enterprises protect business continuity, accelerate innovation, and drive strategic outcomes at scale.

11:35–11:50 PM — Point of View

Further information to come.

11:50 AM–12:50 PM — Lunch Break

12:50–1:30 PM — Sponsor Session

More information to come.

1:30–2:15 PM — Portfolio Extension Deep Dive

Co-Innovating with SAP's Development Services: From AI Ideas to Impact

Speakers:
Sindhu Gangadharan, Head, Customer Innovation Services; MD, SAP Labs India

Discover how SAP Development Services partners with customers to build tailored solutions—from generative AI use cases to agentic AI scenarios. This session includes a real-world example demonstrating how custom AI innovation improved efficiency, decision-making, and measurable business outcomes.

2:15–3:00 PM — Portfolio Extension Deep Dive

Next Generation Application Management for Predictive, Intelligent, and Automated Operations

Speakers:
Lalit Patil, EVP & Chief Technology Officer, SAP
Vinay Hiriganahalli, SVP, Head of Cloud Application Services, SAP

Learn how SAP Application Management is transforming operations from reactive support to predictive, AI-driven optimization. Through artificial intelligence, machine learning, and cognitive automation, organizations can move beyond issue resolution toward proactive performance management and intelligent operations.

3:00–3:15 PM — Break

3:15–3:45 PM — Portfolio Extension Deep Dive

Secure your future with SAP Sovereign Cloud 

Speaker:
Peter Keller, VP, Sovereign Cloud Architecture, SAP

Discover how SAP Sovereign Cloud capabilities can cater to the unique needs of public sector and regulated industries. Learn about these capabilities, which fulfill advancedcompliance requirements across our four digital sovereignty dimensions: data, operational, legal and technical sovereignty. These dimensions address heightened digital sovereigntyneeds enabling companies to leverage the significant benefits of the cloud.  

3:45–4:30 PM — Strategy Session

Build Your SAP Success Strategy: Practical Guidance and Roadmap 2026

Speakers:
Uwe Grigoleit, SVP, Head of SAP Customer Evolution and Portfolio, SAP
Patrick Wells, Global Head of SAP Services Portfolio Evolution, SAP

This practical session helps you translate insights from the day into a clear SAP engagement strategy.

Learn how to:

  • Match business priorities with the right Success Plan

  • Identify ROI drivers

  • Apply best practices for adoption

Build a practical roadmap for 2026

Participants will leave with a template to assess their current setup and define their future SAP success strategy.
Register Now!

Exclusive Bonus: Guided SAP Sapphire Showfloor Tour & F1 Experience

4:30–6:00 PM

Discover Sapphire Like an Insider

Wrap up the day with a curated, expert-led tour of the SAP Sapphire showfloor. Navigating Sapphire can be overwhelming—this guided experience ensures you don’t miss the innovations and demos most relevant to your SAP journey.

Tour highlights include:

  • Exclusive access to the SAP Experience Center

  • Artificial Intelligence innovations

  • Data & Analytics

  • Business Applications

  • Technology Platform

  • Business Transformation

  • Industry Center

  • Services & Support Center

And to finish the day: Experience the Formula 1 racing simulator and connect with SAP experts in an interactive setting. Register Now!

Why You Should Attend

This ASUG pre-conference offers a unique opportunity to learn directly from SAP leaders and customers about the future of services and support.

Participants will gain:

✔ A clear understanding of SAP’s new Services & Support portfolio
✔ Practical insights into AI-driven operations and transformation
✔ Real customer experiences and best practices
✔ Direct access to SAP experts
✔ A structured strategy for maximizing value from SAP investments

Seats are limited — secure your spot and start SAP Sapphire with insights that will shape your transformation journey. Register Now!

entry.title: Why Attend The Fabric of Data & AI: Insights from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone & More
entry.content:
JOIN US AT THE FABRIC OF DATA & AI ON MARCH 24, 2026, 11AM EDT | 11AM CET | 1PM SGT

AI without context is like walking to the car wash.  

You may have seen the viral video: someone asks ChatGPT whether to walk or drive to the car wash. AI analyzes distance, time, and efficiency, then recommends walking. It misses the obvious: you need the car there to wash it. 

It's funny, until you realize the same disconnect is happening in business decisions every day. AI can analyze data, optimize metrics, and execute quickly, but without understanding business context, it can provide technically correct answers that completely miss the mark. 

As organizations deploy AI agents, intelligent applications, and large language models, many are experiencing a critical gap: the data strategies that powered the last decade of innovation weren't designed for autonomous decision-making. 

The Fabric of Data & AI, a virtual summit on March 24, 2026, brings together industry leaders at the forefront of this transformation. Join Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer of SAP Data & Analytics, in conversation with leaders from Google, Ericsson, Vodafone, Team Liquid, Mead Johnson Nutrition, and the Open Data Institute. 

Here are three reasons you should attend. 

1. Understand why yesterday’s data strategies fall short 

For decades, data strategies have focused on extracting data into centralized systems for applications and dashboards. This approach served its purpose for traditional analytics, but it created a problem: the loss of important business context. 

When AI makes critical decisions, such as allocating inventory, prioritizing customers, and managing cash flow, it needs more than just accurate data. It needs to grasp the business relationships, priorities, and trade-offs that determine the right course of action. 

At this summit, you'll discover: 

  • What happens when business context gets lost as data moves across systems 
  • Why AI agents can optimize individually but create problems collectively 
  • How preserving business meaning by design creates a competitive advantage 

2. Learn from leaders solving the same challenges you face

You’ll hear directly from leaders who are building data and AI strategies in their organizations, including: 

  • Irfan Khan, President & Chief Product Officer, SAP Data & Analytics 
  • Jannie Affeld, VP Finance Systems and ERP, Google 
  • Malin Persson, CIO & Head of Enterprise IT, Ericsson 
  • Ricard Rovira Marti, Head of Corporate IT Platforms, Vodafone 
  • Steve Arhancet, Co-CEO and Co-Owner, Team Liquid 
  • Ganesh Sivakumar, Global Chief Data Analytics & AI Officer, Mead Johnson Nutrition 
  • Louise Burke, Chief Executive Officer, Open Data Institute 

Each brings different approaches, unique challenges, and proven outcomes from real-world experience across finance, telecommunications, technology, esports, and consumer goods. 

3. Gain actionable insights for your organization 

Develop a strategic perspective on the decisions that determine AI success. Join us for: 

  • Clarity on the shift: Discover why shared organizational knowledge is becoming a structural requirement for AI success. 
  • The architectural framework: Learn how a business data fabric connects data, processes, and business rules to keep AI grounded in business context. 
  • The integration model: See how technology, process, and culture must evolve together, and why all three are essential for success. 

Whether you lead data and analytics, drive business strategy, or oversee technology implementation, this event provides the insights to successfully navigate this transition. 

Save your spot 

The conversation about data and AI is everywhere, but most of it focuses on what AI can do, not what it needs to succeed. Don't miss this opportunity to learn from the leaders who are building the foundation for reliable AI. 

Register now for The Fabric of Data & AI and join the conversation on March 24, 2026.

entry.title: End-to-End SAP GUI Logon Automation with Codex and Python: Customizing + Reporting
entry.content:
Introduction

Many SAP users still perform repetitive tasks in SAP GUI manually — logging into systems, navigating transactions, adjusting customizing parameters, or extracting reports.With the rise of AI-assisted development and automation frameworks, it is now possible to orchestrate these operations programmatically.

In this article, I present an end-to-end automation scenario that combines:
